speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Isabella Leitner. The interviewer is Raquel Grunwald. The city is New York, USA. It's April 3, 1995. The interview is in English. And we're on tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right, on my right, is my mother, probably newly married, somewhere in the First World War. And that is her brother's second wife, I believe. Yeah. And this is from-- this probably was taken in the town where my mother's father was the rabbi, Hajdunanas, Hungary. This is a grandchild in the family. I don't know who it is. This is my mother's sister, Hencsi [NON-ENGLISH], who has two sons who survived Auschwitz. This is my uncle's second wife, I believe. And this is Sura Schreiber. 'Schreiber' meaning writer. And that was my grandmother's name, because they all writers, Talmudic writers, in her family. And I don't know if you can see the resemblance, but they said I look like her. [LAUGHS] This is my mother, probably not long after they were married. And this is my father's passport picture, many years later. This is my father's father, my oldest sister, and my father's mother. The other lady was my mother's mother. This is my dear, dear, dear grandmother. Pessi [NON-ENGLISH] was her name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Believe it or not, I grew up hearing her telling us the story about the Tiszaezlar affair. Everybody should know what that was. Those were the anti-Semitic blood trials in the 19th century. And she attended those trials. And she told us about it always. And she was the dearest grandmother anyone ever had.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like me. I can barely do right or left either.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my right is my currently younger sister, because they killed my youngest sister. And that's Regina and her friend in junior high, I believe, or high school. And they were doing a play, and this is their costume. This is a Hungarian, traditional Hungarian costume, both of them, as a matter of fact. And this is a school play in Kisvarda, Hungary. Now to my right is Regina again. And next to her is my older sister, who perished as well. Zippy was her name. And then my brother, Philip. Next to him, is Isabella, myself. And next to her is Chica, my currently older sister. And then it's my little sister, Potyo. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then it's my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was the very last photograph that we made at home. We went to a photographer, in order to send the picture to my father, to perhaps aid him in getting papers or some such thing. And I have to turn this around. May I?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to turn it around. Because [SOBS] it's the only sample of my mother's handwriting. This was the last-- [SOBS] she sent this to America, to my father. And it says, it says something to the effect that, to her dearest, to her dear daddy, with great, great love and incredible [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sense of missing him. Mother, and her children. That is the last picture of me at home. And the pictures below are of my son, Richard, my youngest son who is, who was, I don't know, in his teens. And this is when he was born. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when he was born. On the top is my writing in Hungarian and my version of what lice, the size of the lice that we were infested with in Auschwitz. You can tell I cannot draw. And some Hungarian writing that I did in 1945. Below that is Irma-- a picture of Irma Grese, who was our torturer in Auschwitz, in Lager C. And she was hanged in Luneburg, I-- something called Luneburg. And we testified against her. And next to her is Josef Kramer, who was one of the Auschwitz heads of the whole management of Auschwitz. I don't remember what his title was. But one of the Nazis. And next to him is my brother. And he looks he must have been 18 years old. And it is in the ghetto. And he's wearing the yellow star. And I have the picture, and he wants it back. And I can't give it back to him. This is the only one we have from the ghetto. And below that, there is an article in the I believe, that tells of the three sisters who arrived in America, who are the very first survivors, who arrived on the day the war ended. And they're the first survivors of Auschwitz. And this article was done soon after we arrived here. This is an article in the local paper in Binghamton, New York, where my sister lived and still lives. She married a-- the officer on the ship, four weeks after-- a little less than four weeks after we got here. And they settled in Binghamton, where he was born. And that's Isabella, myself. And this is Regina. And this tells of the story of Auschwitz and how we came to America or whatever. But the article is from 1945. This is a, a copy of a large pile of historic document from the FBI that my husband obtained, when the Freedom of Information Act was approved. And it testifies to the fact-- this is only one page of that large file-- that we arrived on the very day the war ended, May 8, 1945. It says we arrived in Newport News, Virginia, aboard the which was a Merchant Marine ship the brought war supplies to Russia and returned with us on the afternoon of May 8, 1945. We gave our testimony starting at 8 AM on March 9, 1945. And that is why it says, subjects arrived Newport News, Virginia, on May 9, 1945, aboard the Because the hearing was on the 9th, though we arrived on a beautiful, sunny, Tuesday afternoon, May 8, 1945. It will be 50 years on May 8.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon after I got here, my emotional state was such that there was no way I can explain anything about Auschwitz to myself. It is-- this, this is how confused I was. And I called this a self portrait. And I didn't sign it until 1983. I finally signed it. And it has my prisoner-- prison number on it, which I wore. We were not tattooed. Most of the Hungarian Jews were not. And I had a dog tag in my neck. And the number was 79212.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I put the number on it. And according to psychiatrists, as confused as I was, as, as totally bewildered as I was, the red marks in the drawing indicate the life force. That despite this bad emotional state, I still wanted to live. So it's there for the future. I guess I'm going to give it to my children. And I hope they will-- you know, that it will be here for a long while.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's probably as authentic a picture of the soul of a Auschwitz survivor as can be. At least it speaks to me. I hope it speaks to others as well. If it pleases anyone, this-- I stopped smoking. Richard made me stop. He's the one who's smoking today. And this is my sister, Regina. And this is her then fiance, Bob Weber. And I believe this picture was taken in 1949. And this is our oldest son Peter, who lives in Portland, Oregon. And he's a chiropractor [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a wonderful man. And this is the meaning to my life, [SOBS] my two children Peter and Richard. And when-- this is my victory over Hitler. [SOBBING] I stood in front of the crematorium, condemned to death, and brought forth lives, such as these. I think, just seeing it, this picture would be enough to kill Hitler. That I-- though I lost most of my family, in some glorious way, I won. These are my children. And I love them so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, my name is Irving Leitner. And Isabella and I have been married, lo, the 39 years. We knew each other for three years before that. And it seems we've always been together. And this is one of the byproducts on my right, my younger son Richard. And it's been a joy to have him. And I have another son, Peter, whom you've met by the photographs. And I guess I'll let Richard introduce himself. By the way, I happen to be a writer also. And I've written a number of books, cooperated with Isabella on her memoirs. I've written plays and have written a play which had its world premiere in St. Petersburg, Russia in 1993, based upon the book And it ran for six months in St. Petersburg. And hopefully it will receive an American production shortly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, I'm Richard. And I'm the son of these two. And I'm a performing artist. I do voice overs. I've appeared in a number of my father's plays. Actually, prior to the world premiere of that he was discussing, I was involved in a staged reading of the same play, at the 92nd Street Y in New York, at the American Jewish Theater, where we did a reading with Colleen Dewhurst and others. I've also done a number of readings with my mother, from her works, publicly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] participating in commemorations of the experiences of, of my family during the Holocaust. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll turn it over to the party of the first part.
